options.a: The numbers are revision counters — `(2)` is a newer version that replaces `(1)`.
options.b: They are distinct overloads of the same function; the description below uses the numbers to refer to each one individually.
options.c: `(1)` is the public version and `(2)` is the implementation detail you should not rely on.
options.d: `(2)` is only available on certain platforms.

explanation_md: cppreference numbers the overloads on a page so the prose can talk about each one separately — `(1) returns the smaller of `a` and `b`; (2) returns the smallest element of `ilist`'. Both are part of the standard and both are usable; which one your call invokes depends on the arguments you pass.

From the documented behaviour, `std::min(7, 3)` returns the smaller value `3`. For `std::min(4, 4)`, the documentation states it returns the first argument when the two are equivalent, which is `4`. The two values are printed separated by a single space on one line.

prompt_md: A cppreference entry for a function says `(since C++20)` at the top. Your project compiles with `-std=c++17`. What is the practical consequence?

options.a: The function will work but emit a warning.
options.b: The function does not exist in your compilation mode; calling it will fail to compile.
options.c: The function works at runtime but cannot be inlined.
options.d: The function is forbidden in production code.

explanation_md: `(since C++20)` markers tell you the entry was added in that standard. Compiling against an earlier standard means the declaration is simply not visible — the name is undeclared and the call is rejected at compile time. Matching the reference to your `-std=` flag is part of using cppreference correctly.

options.b: The call accidentally selects the initializer-list overload of `std::min` instead of the two-argument overload — the comment says one thing, the code does another.
options.c: `std::min` does not exist in C++; this is a typo for `std::minimum`.
options.d: `std::min` requires both arguments to be `const T&` references.

explanation_md: `{ 7 }` is a single `std::initializer_list<int>`, so the call resolves to the initializer-list overload that takes a list and returns its smallest element. It compiles and prints `7`, but it is not the two-argument overload the comment promised. The fix is to write `std::min(7, 7)` (two arguments) if that is what you wanted, or to read the reference's overload list before writing the call.
